cdc_causes.name: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

items: Myth busted: star color is not cosmetic—it is a major clue to surface temperature.
Myth busted: not every rock is a mineral. Minerals meet specific natural, chemical, and structural criteria.
Myth busted: devices do not make energy vanish; they convert it from one form to another.
Myth busted: spiders are arachnids, not insects. They have eight legs and two main body sections.
Myth busted: ALARA does not mean zero radiation; it means exposure as low as reasonably achievable.
Myth busted: fish slime is not dirt. The mucus layer helps protect skin from injury, parasites, and infection.
Myth busted: tree rings show more than age—their widths can record changing growing conditions.
Myth busted: dark desert soil crust is alive, fragile, and slow to recover after being stepped on.
Myth busted: honey bees are not the only pollinators; many insects and other animals move pollen.
Myth busted: there is no one-size-fits-all horse diet. Feed depends on workload, age, health, and condition.
Myth busted: not every extinguisher is safe for cooking-oil fires. Class K is designed for commercial kitchens.
Myth busted: barriers are not optional around blood. Gloves reduce exposure to bloodborne pathogens.
Myth busted: do not scrub a knocked-out permanent tooth. Handle the crown and get dental help immediately.
Myth busted: if clothes catch fire, do not run—stop, drop, and roll, then cool the burn.
Myth busted: public health is not only hospital care; it tracks population patterns to guide prevention.
Myth busted: a browser cookie is not a program. It is a small piece of data stored for a website.
Myth busted: HTTP is not the whole internet; it is a protocol used to exchange resources on the web.
Myth busted: GPS positioning comes from satellites, not cellular towers, though apps may use mobile data for maps.
Myth busted: binary 0 and 1 represent two states; they are not merely tiny decimal numbers.
Myth busted: CAD is more than electronic drawing; it supports design, modification, analysis, and documentation.
